segments.s736-g17: A poetic enumerative prepositional phrase: the repeated preposition ز (z) = “from” introduces a list of lands joined by و (and). Each place name follows the usual Persian order (preposition then noun). سقلاب is an obscure/place-name here; خزر refers to the Khazars (a people/region) rather than the Caspian Sea.
segments.s736-g18: Main predicative clause: چنین (thus/so) modifies ارجمند (esteemed), and آمد is the past tense verb “came / became” (here stative: “came to be”). آن بوم و بر is the subject phrase “that bōm and bar” — bōm (homeland) with the paired بر (here idiomatic with بوم to mean domain/territory). Word order places the degree adverb first, adjective then verb, then the demonstrative + noun phrase.
segments.s736-g19: Parallel enumerative construction using repeated چه (che) in the sense “whatever / whether … or …” to list qualities. Here چه + noun functions idiomatically: چه مردی = whether manhood/manliness, چه دانش = whether learning, چه پرهیز و دین = whether piety and religion. The series is coordinated by و (and).
segments.s736-g20: Result clause: ز یزدان is a source phrase “from God,” شما را is the object with the emphatic/definite object marker را, رسید is the past verb “reached / came,” and آفرین is the thing that reached them (here ‘praise’/‘acclamation’/‘blessing’). Word order is source – object – verb – object (the final noun is the received item).
segments.s736-g21: Temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو (chū) = “when.” کار آمدم combines کار (the affair/matter) with آمدم (I/it came); پیش یارم is a prepositional phrase “before/in the presence of my friend.” The final بدی is problematic in the transmitted orthography: in classical verse it can reflect a contracted/archaic verbal form (e.g. an elided second-person past) or be read as the noun “harm/evil.” The syntax here treats بدی as a predicate referring back to the addressee, so the exact lexical identification is irregular/archaic (see senses mapping).
segments.s736-g22: Purpose/benefit phrase with بهر (bahar) = “for / for the sake of,” followed by دانشی (knowledge, in an oblique/construct sense ‘for knowledge’). غمگسارم is a compound meaning “my consoler / my sympathizer” (here the person who comforts), and the final بدی repeats the same irregular form as above — functionally a predicate addressing the addressee but not matching the noun senses supplied, so treated as irregular/archaic here.
segments.s736-g23: Degree + result: چنان (so/thus) modifies شاد گشتم (I became happy — گشتم is the past of گشتن used here as 'became'). ز پیوند تو is the reason/cause phrase “because of your bond/union” with the preposition ز = “from/because of” and the possessor تو following پیوند.
segments.s736-g24: Demonstrative/specifier + adjectival phrase modifying فرزند تو: بدین (classical/poetic 'with/by this' = 'به ‌+ ‌این' contracted) introduces the demonstrative; پر هنر is an adjectival compound 'full of art/skill' (پر = full (of) + هنر = skill/excellence), پاک is another adjective 'pure,' and فرزند تو is the head noun with the possessive pronoun تو. Note the classical ordering: adjectives and modifiers precede the noun, and the possessor follows it.
segments.s736-g97: This prepositional phrase describes what each item was set with: به + noun phrase. Here به functions as a preposition roughly 'with/on/at' leading the phrase گوهر بیاگنده ('an unblemished/undefiled jewel'). هر یک چو سنگ ('each one like a stone') is a simile appended after the noun; چو = 'like/as' links هر یک (each one) to سنگ (stone). Word order is poetic: preposition + noun + modifiers + simile.
segments.s736-g98: نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/set'. It governs the following prepositional-object: بر (on/upon) + هر یکی (each one) + مهر تنگ (a close/tight seal). مهر is used in the sense 'seal/stamp', modified by the adjective تنگ 'tight/close' placed after the noun, as is normal in Persian adjective-noun ordering here reversed for rhythm.
segments.s736-g99: بران is an adverbial/prepositional particle 'upon/for/thereon' introducing the matter; it frames 'هر یکی دانه‌ها' = 'each one of the beads/pearls'. صد هزار is the numeric value 'one hundred thousand' placed immediately after the quantified noun phrase for emphasis (typical Persian word order in poetic listing).
segments.s736-g100: بها بود is the predicate 'was the price/value' (بها = value/price, بود = was). The locative/record phrase بر دفتر شهریار ('on the king's book/register') follows to indicate where it was entered or recorded; دفتر شهریار is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's ledger'.
segments.s736-g101: بیاورد is the past tense verb 'he brought' (3rd person). The object follows: سیصد شتر ('three hundred camels'). سرخ موی is a compound adjective phrase 'red‑haired' where سرخ (red) modifies موی (hair) and together qualifies the camels (i.e., 'red‑haired camels').
segments.s736-g102: This string lists additional epithets: سیه چشم 'black‑eyed' (adjective + noun), و 'and' links the next descriptor, آراسته 'adorned'. راه جوی is a compound: راه (road) + جوی (literally 'seeking'/'searching' here) giving the sense 'fit for the road' or 'road‑seeking' — i.e. arrayed/suited for travel. The adjectives follow the noun they describe (poetic compaction).
segments.s736-g103: This phrase marks the indirect object/recipient and amount: مران is a proper name (or group name) 'Meran'; هر یکی را uses هر یکی + the object marker را to indicate 'to each one'. درم دو هزار is the numeral phrase 'two thousand dirhams' (درم = dirham). The order places recipient and amount before the verb, a common Persian sequencing.
segments.s736-g104: بها داده is the verbal phrase 'has given (as) value' or simply 'gave'; here بها functions as 'value/reward' and داده is the past participle/verb 'given'. بد نامور شهریار is the subject phrase 'the ill‑famed king' — بد + نامور negates/qualifies نامور ('famed'), producing 'ill‑famed/notorious', placed before شهریار (king) in poetic order; the subject follows the verb for emphasis.
segments.s736-g41: بما بر ز دین کهن ننگ نیست is a negative copular clause meaning “there is no shame for us from/about the ancient religion.” بما (prep. phrase) = “for us”; بر ... ز = a compounded prepositional sense “on/account of / because of”; دین کهن is the genitive phrase “the ancient religion”; ننگ نیست is the predicate “(there) is no shame.” Word order is typical older Persian: prepositional phrase + reason + predicate.
segments.s736-g42: به گیتی به از دین هوشنگ نیست states a comparative: “In the world there is nothing better than the religion of Hushang.” به گیتی = locative/scene-setting “in the world”; به از ... نیست is the comparative construction “there is not (any) thing better than ...”; دین هوشنگ = “Hushang’s religion.” Note the elliptical subject (’nothing’) understood from the negative predicate.
segments.s736-g43: همه داد و نیکی و شرمست و مهر lists moral qualities: همه introduces the list “all/they are”; داد و نیکی = “justice and goodness”; شرمست (an adjectival form) = “modest/ashamed (in the sense of modesty)”; و مهر = “and love/affection.” The copula linking (are) is implied, common in poetic Persian lists.
segments.s736-g44: نگه کردن اندر شمار سپهر is an infinitival/nominal phrase meaning “to keep (them) in the reckoning of the heavens” or “to count among the heavens.” نگه کردن uses کردن as a light verb (= “to keep/hold”); اندر شمار سپهر = prepositional phrase “within the count/number of the sky (heavens).” The whole phrase modifies or explains how those qualities are treated.
segments.s736-g45: به هستی یزدان نیوشان ترم is a comparative personal statement: به هستی یزدان = “toward/with respect to God’s being/existence”; نیوشان (from نیوشیدن) here means “to hear/regard/attend to” (poetic use), and ترم is an archaic comparative predicative meaning “I am more (inclined).” Together: “I am more inclined to regard/hear of God’s existence.”
segments.s736-g46: همیشه سوی داد کوشان ترم repeats the comparative structure to state a disposition: همیشه = “always”; سوی داد کوشان = “toward justice, striving” (سوی = toward; کوشان = “striving” used adjectivally/participial); ترم again is the comparative/predicative “I am more.” The clause means “I am ever more eager/striving toward justice.”
segments.s736-g47: ندانیم انباز و پیوند و جفت is a negated clause: ندانیم (negative present/plural) = “we do not know.” The speaker lists three terms not understood or unexplained: انباز (an obscure/obsolete word), پیوند “bond/link,” and جفت “pair.” The list is the direct object of ندانیم.
segments.s736-g48: نگردد نهان و نگردد نهفت uses present-future verbal forms in the sense “do not become / are not made hidden.” نگردد / نگردد (subjunctive/epistolary-form of گشتن with negative) = “does not become” or “is not made”; نهان and نهفت are near-synonymous adjectives/nouns meaning “hidden/concealed.” The pair asserts that these things are not hidden nor remain hidden.
segments.s736-g1: چویک ماه شد is an archaic temporal clause: چو + یک (contracted to چویک) = 'when one/a month had become/passed'. ماه is 'month' and شد is the past/preterite 'became/passed'. نامه پاسخ نوشت is a typical SOV sequence: نامهِ پاسخ ('a reply-letter') as object before the verb نوشت ('wrote', 3rd singular past).
segments.s736-g2: سخنهای ('the sayings/words', plural) is the object; با introduces an instrumental phrase ('with') and links to مغز و فرخ ('with brain/wit and with good-fortune/auspiciousness') as modifiers describing the manner/content of the words. The clause ends with the verb نوشت ('wrote') repeating the action of composition.
segments.s736-g3: سرنامه is the speaking noun 'the heading/title'; گفت is the verb 'said' (classical narrative past). آفرین مهان is an exclamatory phrase ('Bravo, guests') functioning as direct speech or an uttered blessing; مهان is vocative/plural 'guests, visitors'.
segments.s736-g4: This line uses an archaic/poetic ordering and a doubled باد with two senses: the first باد is an optative particle meaning 'may' (a wish), and the second باد is the noun 'wind'. بران ('upon that' / 'for them') introduces the beneficiary of the wish. کو begins a relative/archaic clause 'which/that' and دارد جهان ('the world has') completes the clause: literally 'May it be upon them that which the world has as wind.'
segments.s736-g5: بد و نیک ('bad and good') are coordinated objects or predicates; بیند is a literary present/gnomic form of 'to see' (here 'perceives' or 'is seen'), and ز (poetic form of از) marks source: 'from Yezdān (یزدان) پاک' = 'from the pure God.' Word order is typical classical Persian where the source phrase follows the verb.
segments.s736-g6: وزو here is an archaic contraction (classical spelling of و ز او = 'and from him') introducing the next clause; دارد is the present/neutral 'has' (3rd sing.), اندر جهان is a locative 'in the world', and بیم و باک are two synonymous nouns 'fear and dread' forming the object/complement. The clause therefore reads 'and from him the world has fear and dread.'
segments.s736-g7: کند آفرین is a verbal phrase 'he makes/utters praise' (کند = 'does/makes', a simple present/past-form verb in narrative usage). بر introduces the indirect object 'to/toward' خداوند مهر: خداوند is 'lord' and مهر here is a proper name/deity (Mithra). The whole phrase means 'he praises the lord Mithra.'
segments.s736-g8: کزین گونه = 'from/through this manner' (کز = from + این 'this'; گونه = 'kind/manner'); بر پای داشتن is an idiom meaning 'to stand/ be upheld' where پای ('foot/base') is the ground of support. دارد is 'has' and سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the element being supported. Literal order is somewhat inverted in classical style: 'for by this manner the heavens have a footing.'
segments.s736-g9: نخست آنک (nəxst ānak) is an archaic/poetic opening: نَخست = 'first' and آنک (آنگه/آن‌که) introduces the clause meaning 'that' or 'inasmuch as/because'. کردی is 2nd-person singular past of کردن 'to do' (you did), and the object ستایش مرا (praise me) appears before the verb — an inversion frequent in classical Persian poetry (object + verb). The clitic م here stands for the enclitic pronoun 'me' (مرا), and را is the definite/object marker attached after it.
segments.s736-g10: به نامه نمودی is a second, coordinated clause. به + نامه = 'into/in a letter' (به here = 'into/towards'), نمودی is 2nd-person past of نمودن/نماوردن 'to show/make (known)', and نیایش مرا is 'my prayer' with the same enclitic pronoun + object marker (م + را). The verb نمودی governs the phrase 'نیایش مرا' as its object; the whole clause means 'you set my prayer down/shown in a letter.'
segments.s736-g11: بدانستم is the 1st-person past 'I knew' (ب + دانستم in classical spelling) and functions here as 'I knew it/this.' و is the coordinating 'and.' شاد گشتم is a light verb construction: شاد (happy) + گَشتم (1st-person past of گشتن 'to become/turn'), giving 'I became glad.' بدَان at the end is an adverbial phrase 'by/at that' (literary form meaning 'because of that' or 'with respect to that'), linking this clause to the following noun phrase.
segments.s736-g12: سخن گفتن is a noun + infinitive/masdar construction meaning 'the speaking/saying' or 'speech.' تاجور is a noun 'merchant/trader' and بخردان is a vocative or descriptive plural/adjective 'to/for the wise' (بخردان = 'O wise ones' / 'to the wise'); here the phrase names the speech being referred to: 'the merchant's speech addressed to the wise.' In classical poetry such noun phrases can stand appositive to the prior clause (the thing that made him glad).
segments.s736-g13: پذیرفتم is 1st-person past 'I accepted/received.' آن نامور گنج تو is the object phrase: آن = 'that/the', نامور = 'renowned/famed', گنج = 'treasure', and تو = the possessive 'your' (postposed as in Persian). The verb precedes its object here (classical inversion), but the meaning is straightforward: 'I accepted that famed treasure of yours.'
segments.s736-g14: نخواهم که is a negative desiderative: نخواهم (literally 'I will not/wish not') + که 'that', introducing a subordinate clause expressing what the speaker does not want. چندان بود = 'be so much' (چندان = 'to that extent/so much', بود = past/subjunctive-like 'be' used here as a jussive/optative), and رنج تو = 'your suffering/hardship.' The whole line means 'I do not want that your suffering be so great.'
segments.s736-g15: ازیرا is a literary/archaic connective (here functioning like 'therefore' or 'for that reason'); جهاندار is a compound title 'world-holder/sovereign' (a title of God or king), یزدان پاک = 'the pure God' (یزدان = God/divine; پاک = pure). The three words together form a reverential subject: 'Therefore the sovereign, pure God...' (archaic epithets and word order are typical of epic Persian).
segments.s736-g16: برآورد is a past/telic verb meaning 'raised/caused to be placed' or more generally 'brought about/raised up.' بوم تو را = 'your land/homeland' with را marking the definite object. بر + سماک here means 'upon/to the sky/heavens' (بر as 'onto/upon' + سماک as a lofty place/name). The clause reads '...raised your land upon the heavens.'
segments.s736-g129: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb 'now' modifying the whole clause. داستان کهن is a noun phrase 'the old/ancient tale' (داستان + the adjective کهن). نو functions as a resultative/secondary predicate meaning 'new' and combines with the verb کنیم ('we make/let us make') to mean 'make ... new.' کنیم is the present-subjunctive/hortative first-person plural of کردن used here as 'let us make' or 'we will make' in poetic narration.
segments.s736-g130: سخنهای شیرین is a noun phrase 'sweet words/tales' (سخن + plural suffix های, modified by شیرین). The coordinating و links that phrase to خسرو so that both are governed by the same verb کنيم; کنيم is again the hortative 'let us make/compose.' In this compact poetic construction خسرو stands as a coordinated direct object (either the proper name Khosrow or the common noun 'king') and the verb applies to both coordinated items; the exact relation (e.g. 'speak of the king' vs. 'praise the king') is expressed by the verb/context rather than a preposition.
segments.s736-g105: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing a partitive list. دیبای چینی is a compound noun 'Chinese brocade' (noun + adjectival origin). صد و چل هزار is a numeric phrase 'one hundred and forty thousand' formed as 'صد و چل' (100 + 40) with هزار (thousand) implied as the multiplier; the whole functions as the quantified object of ز.
segments.s736-g106: ازان چند is a partitive/explanatory phrase 'of those some' (ازان = 'of those' or 'from that'), used to pick out a subset of the previously mentioned items. زربفت گوهرنگار is a noun phrase 'gold‑woven (zarbaf) and jewel‑adorned', where زربفت (gold‑brocade) is modified by the compound adjective گوهرنگار 'jeweled/gem‑inscribed'.
segments.s736-g107: دگر modifies the numeral and means 'another' or 'additional'. پانصد is 'five hundred'. در خوشاب here functions as a locative phrase 'in khoshab / in sweet water' (در = 'in'); the copula بود ('was/were') follows, forming a past‑state clause 'there were another five hundred in sweet water.'
segments.s736-g108: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause 'that / namely'. هر دانه‌ای is the subject 'each bead/pearl' (دانه + indefinite suffix -ای). قطره آب بود is the predicate 'was a drop of water' with قطره آب as a nominal predicate and بود as the past copula; the construction is essentially 'each bead was a drop of water.'
segments.s736-g109: صد و شست is a poetic numeral meaning 'one hundred and sixty' (صد 'one hundred' + و + شست 'sixty' — شست is an archaic/poetic form for sixty). یاقوت is the counted noun 'ruby(s)'. چون introduces a simile 'like', and ناردان is the comparison image (here intended as 'pomegranate‑seeds'); the phrase reads '160 rubies like pomegranate‑seeds.'
segments.s736-g110: پسندیده is a passive participle/adjectival past participle meaning 'approved/esteemed' (here adjectival). مردم کاردان functions as the agent or the approving group 'the discerning/skilled people' (مردم 'people' modified by کاردان 'skillful, expert'). Together the phrase means 'approved (or prized) by discerning people.'
segments.s736-g111: This is a coordinate list of origins. ز introduces the list 'from', followed by national/ethnic adjectives هندی (Indian) and چینی (Chinese) linked by و ('and'). Then از بربری adds 'from the Berber/barbarian (sources)'; the alternation of ز and از is stylistic for listing multiple origins.
segments.s736-g112: The list continues: ز مصری 'from Egyptian' (مصری = Egyptian), و 'and', از جامه پهلوی 'from Pahlavi garments' — جامه is 'garment/cloth' and پهلوی here functions as an ethnographic/period adjective referring to Pahlavi (Parthian/Pahlavi style). The whole segment names further sources/types of cloth or goods.
segments.s736-g25: که here introduces a subordinate/result clause meaning 'that/so that.' کهتر is a predicative adjective 'lesser/inferior.' نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative (3rd person singular) of بودن used to express a wished-for/result state ('may he not be / that he not be'); together 'که کهتر نباشد' = 'that he may not be inferior.' به + noun in classical Persian can mark comparison or relation ('to/than' his son), so به فرزندِ خویش = 'to/than his own son.' خویش is the enclitic possessive 'his/one's own.'
segments.s736-g26: ببوم is an archaic/poetic 1st-person form expressing an optative/volitive ('may I kiss / may I press (to my lips)') — here functioning as the speaker's action or wish. The following و is the coordinating 'and.' بر is a preposition 'on/upon' that introduces the following noun phrase; the second و is another coordinator before پاک پیوند خویش. پاک qualifies پیوند ('pure bond'), and خویش is the possessive enclitic 'my/his own' referring back to the kin/lineage; together پاک پیوند خویش = 'the pure bond of my kin.'
segments.s736-g27: همه مهتران (subject + quantifier) + پشت (noun 'back') + برگاشتند (verb). The phrase literally = 'all the elders turned (their) backs.' برگاشتند is simple past, 3rd person plural; پشت stands as the thing turned (object) without an explicit possessive pronoun (context supplies 'their').
segments.s736-g28: مرا (accusative 'me' marked by را) + در جهان خوار (prepositional phrase 'in the world [as] despised') + بگذاشتند (past verb 'they left/placed'). The object precedes the verb (typical Persian order); خوار is a predicative adjective describing the state in which they left 'me'. بگذاشتند is 3rd person plural past meaning 'they left/let [someone] be'.
segments.s736-g29: تو (vocative/you) + تنها (adverb 'alone/only') + بجای پدر (prepositional phrase 'in place of the father') + بودیم (past copula 'we were'). بجای governs the noun 'پدر' to express 'in the stead of'; بودیم is 1st person plural past — here the speaker uses a plural verb form to refer to the speaker's group acting in the father's stead while addressing 'تو'.
segments.s736-g30: همان (demonstrative 'that one') + از پدر بیشتر (comparative 'more than the father') + بودیم (past copula 'we were'). The comparative construction uses از + noun + بیشتر to mean 'more than X.' همان picks out the person just spoken of; بودیم again is past plural copula tying the predication to that referent.
segments.s736-g31: تو را (object 'you' marked by را) + همچنان دارم (adverb 'still' + present 1st person singular 'I have') + اکنون که شاه (temporal/conjunctive 'now that the king ...'). دارم is present-tense 'I have/keep'; اکنون که introduces the subordinate clause that follows (که = 'that/when/since').
segments.s736-g32: پدر (subject 'father') + بیند (classical/polite present/subjunctive 3rd sing. of 'to see') + آزاده و نیک‌خواه (two predicate adjectives 'noble-hearted and well‑wishing'). بیند here functions as a present/optative form used in classical Persian; the adjectives after it describe the referent seen (پدر).
segments.s736-g57: که ... گوید که introduces a reported clause: the first که is the complementizer 'that' and گوید is the classical 3rd-person present/punctual form 'says' used to introduce reported speech. The second که begins the reported content: فرزند یزدان is a genitive construction 'the son of God' (noun + possessor), and بد اوی is a predicative phrase where بد = 'bad/evil' and اوی is an archaic enclitic/pronoun referring back to the subject ('he' / 'is he'); together it means 'that the Son of God is evil.'
segments.s736-g58: بران دار is an old-fashioned sequence meaning roughly 'on that gallows' (بران = 'on/thereon/that', دار = 'gallows / tree' here). بر کشته is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the slain' (بر + کشته), while خندان is a participial/adjectival form 'laughing' describing the action (he is 'laughing over the slain'). The final بد اوی repeats the predicative pattern 'he is evil' or 'how bad he is' — here functioning as a judgement on the person being hung or the attitude described.
segments.s736-g59: چو here means 'when'; پور پدر is a genitive-like juxtaposition 'the son (پور) of the father (پدر)' i.e. 'the son'; رفت is the simple past 'went'; سوی پدر is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward (his) father' with سوی + noun. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal 'when' + subject phrase + verb + direction.
segments.s736-g60: تو is the vocative/emphatic 'you'. اندوه is the noun 'sorrow' used as the object of the following negative imperative; این چوب پوده 'this patched (or woven-patch) stick' is a compound noun phrase (این = 'this', چوب = 'stick', پوده = 'patch/weft' → 'patched stick' or 'patched bit of wood'). مخور is the negative imperative form of خوردن 'to eat' used idiomatically with اندوه: 'مخور اندوه' = 'do not take/harbor sorrow' → 'do not grieve.'
segments.s736-g61: ز قیصر is a poetical prepositional phrase 'from Caesar' (ز = 'from'). چو = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. بیهوده آمد سخن literally 'a speech/word came in vain' where بیهوده 'in vain/pointless' modifies آمد سخن 'a message/word arrived' — together: 'when a futile message came from Caesar.'
segments.s736-g62: بخندد is the classical 3rd-person form 'he laughs' (here general/presentive). برین کار = بر + ین(=این) + کار is 'at/on this matter/affair' (بر = at/on, ین = orthographic variant of این = 'this', کار = 'affair/deed'). مرد کهن is the subject 'the old man' (مرد = man, کهن = old/ancient). Word order places the verb before its prepositional object for rhetorical effect, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s736-g63: همان is the emphatic demonstrative 'that same'. دار here is the noun 'gallows' or 'tree' (in Christian context 'the cross'), followed by عیسی 'Jesus' in a genitive relation: 'the tree/gallows of Jesus'. نیرزد is the negative present 'is not worth / does not merit', and به رنج (به + رنج) is the prepositional phrase 'to/for suffering' — together: 'that very gallows of Jesus is not worth the suffering.'
segments.s736-g64: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/for that', شاهان is the plural subject 'kings', نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن 'they placed/put', and آن را is the definite object 'it' with the object marker را. به گنج (به + گنج) indicates the sphere/means 'with/in treasure' — 'kings have placed it with treasure' (i.e., adorned or endowed it with riches).
segments.s736-g73: به + recipient (شیروی) + بخشیدم (past 1st sg): here به marks the beneficiary/recipient ('to/for Shiru'), and بخشیدم is the speaker's past action meaning 'I bestowed/gave'. این برده رنج is a noun phrase 'this slave (who has suffered)' with رنج qualifying the slave as one who has endured hardship; word order (demonstrative before noun, qualifier after) is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s736-g74: پی افگندم او را یکی تازه گنج: پی افگندم is a compound verbal idea 'I cast/sent (someone) after / in pursuit of'; او را is the direct object with را after the pronoun (topic/accusative marker), and یکی تازه گنج is the goal phrase 'a certain/new treasure' (یکی = 'a/certain', تازه = 'new', گنج = 'treasure'). The verb is 1st-person past, and the sequence shows that the speaker dispatched the person to seek the treasure.
segments.s736-g75: ز روم و ز ایران پر اندیشه‌ام: ز ... ز introduces the two domains 'from/about Rome and Iran'; پر here is an adjectival predicate 'full (of)', and اندیشه‌ام is 'my thought(s)' with the possessive suffix -ام. The word order places the locative/about phrases first, then the predicate 'full', then the possessed noun.
segments.s736-g76: شب تیره اندیشه شد پیشه‌ام: this is a nominal clause where 'شب تیره اندیشه' ('a dark/gloomy night of thought') is the subject; شد is the past of شدن 'to become', and پیشه‌ام ('my profession/occupation') is the predicate with possessive -ام. The construction means 'X became my trade/lot', i.e. the speaker's habitual state.
segments.s736-g77: بترسم که شیروی گردد بلند: بترسم is present-tense 'I fear' (subjunctive-flavored statement), که introduces the subordinate clause. گردد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of شدن/گردیدن 'become', and بلند is the predicate adjective 'lofty/mighty'. The clause expresses the speaker's fear about a possible change of state.
segments.s736-g78: رساند به روم و به ایران گزند: رساند (here) functions as 'he will bring/inflict' (deliver/produce) and گزند is its object 'harm/damage'; the two به-phrases mark the targets 'to Rome and to Iran'. In context this continues the subordinate clause introduced by که, stating the feared consequence.
segments.s736-g79: نخست اندر آید ز سلم بزرگ: نخست 'first/at first' is adverbial, اندر آید (آید with the older locative اندر) means 'will come in/enter' or 'will arise', and ز + سلم بزرگ indicates the source 'from Salm the Great' (سلم here is treated as a source/name or locus). The verb is third-person present-future sense used in narration.
segments.s736-g80: ز اسکندر آن کینه دار سترگ: z introduces another source 'from Alexander'; آن 'that' points to a specific figure, and کینه‌دار is a compound meaning 'one who bears hatred' (کینه = hatred, دار here functions as the '-possessing' element), while سترگ is an emphatic/epithet meaning 'mighty/great' in poetic usage. The whole phrase characterizes Alexander as a powerful holder of a grudge.
segments.s736-g113: ز + چیزی begins a prepositional noun phrase 'from something'. که introduces a relative clause modifying چیزی; خیزد is a 3rd‑person present/present-subjunctive (classical/pedestrian form of خاستن) meaning 'arises/appears'. The second ز marks origin inside the relative clause ('arises from any country'). Note the Persian order: noun (چیزی) followed by its relative clause.
segments.s736-g114: که continues the relative clause 'that...'; چونان is a demonstrative/simile 'such/as such'; نبد is an archaic/poetic past negative ('was not' / 'does not exist') used here to say that no counterpart exists elsewhere; در جهان دیگری literally 'in another world' — the clause therefore means 'that such does not exist in any other world.'
segments.s736-g115: فرستاد is the simple past (3rd person singular) 'he sent/dispatched'. سیصد (implied here) + شتروار بار functions as the object: شتروار is a classifier/compound referring to 'in camel-loads' (i.e. loads measured by camels) and بار = 'load/cargo'. The phrase therefore means 'three hundred camel-loads of cargo.'
segments.s736-g116: از ایران marks origin 'from Iran' (از = from). بر before قیصر here marks the recipient/direction ('to/toward Caesar') — older Persian uses بر for the indirect object. نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned/illustrious' modifying قیصر.
segments.s736-g117: یکی is the indefinite/numeral 'one' (here introducing a single robe). خلعت is the traditional 'robe of honour' and افگند is the past form of افکندن 'to cast/throw' used idiomatically for placing or bestowing a robe on someone; بر خانگی = 'upon a householdly person' (خانگی used adjectivally as a recipient, 'a domestic/household person' or 'householder').
segments.s736-g118: فزون‌تر is the comparative 'more/exceeding'. ز here introduces the standard comparative ablative 'more than' (فزون‌تر ز ...). خویشی و بیگانگی are the two nouns compared ('kinship and alienation/foreignness'), so the phrase means 'surpassing kinship and estrangement' (i.e. generosity beyond considerations of kin or stranger).
segments.s736-g119: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to items previously listed. The nouns جامه, تخت, اسب are a simple asyndetic list 'garment(s), throne, horse'. ستام (from the root ستاد/ستا) here functions as a nominalized idea 'praise' or 'praised things' added to the list in a poetic register.
segments.s736-g120: ز پوشیدنیها = 'from among the wearables/garments' (ز = from, پوشیدنیها = clothing/items to be worn). که بردیم نام is a relative clause 'whose name we took/which we named/which we mentioned' (بردیم is 1st person plural past 'we took/recorded' and نام = 'name'); together: 'from the wearables whose names we mentioned.'
segments.s736-g121: بدینسان چنین is a pair of adverbials meaning 'thus / in this way' placed before the noun phrase. صد شتر is the numeral + noun 'a hundred camels' serving as the direct object, and بارکرد is the past-tense verb 'loaded' (compound poetic form of بار کردن) placed after the object — standard older/poetic Persian word order here (adverbials + object + verb). The subject is implicit ('they').
segments.s736-g122: از آن ده شتربار is a prepositional phrase 'from those ten camel-loads' (از = 'from', آن = 'those/that', ده before the compound noun شتربار). دینار کرد is a verb phrase where دینار (dinar) is the direct object and کرد ('made/did') functions to mean 'turned into / counted as / converted to' — a light-verb construction common in Persian; the subject remains implicit.
segments.s736-g123: ببخشید is a past plural verb 'they bestowed / they gave' (here not 'forgave'). بر introduces the beneficiary 'to' or 'upon', so بر فیلسوفان درم means 'dirhams to the philosophers' — the indirect object (فیلسوفان) is marked by بر and the money (درم) is the thing bestowed. The verb precedes the indirect-object phrase in the lineated poetic order.
segments.s736-g124: ز (poetic variant of از) begins an enumerative phrase 'from / out of' — ز دینار و هرگونه‌ای بیش و کم lists other monies and amounts: دینار 'dinar', و 'and', هرگونه‌ای 'of any kind / of every sort', followed by بیش و کم 'more and less' meaning 'in varying amounts'. The whole unit modifies the giving: they gave dirhams, dinars, and sums of every kind, large and small.
segments.s736-g125: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed / they went'; شادان is an adverbial/adjectival predicate 'joyfully / rejoicing' modifying the subject. ازان مرز وبوم is a prepositional phrase 'from that border and land' (ازان = 'from that', مرز = 'border', و + بوم = 'and the homeland'); the phrase marks the place they left.
segments.s736-g126: به نزدیک قیصر is a directional phrase 'toward/near the Caesar' (به = 'toward/to', نزدیک = 'near / toward the presence of'); ز ایران is 'from Iran'. بروم (here the lexical sense 'to go') functions as the verb 'they went' in this poetic narrative line — an older/poetic verbal form whose surface shape matches the first-person present/subjunctive in modern Persian but is used narratively here to indicate motion away toward the Caesar.
segments.s736-g127: همه مهتران is 'all the chiefs/elders' (همه = 'all', مهتران = 'great ones/chiefs' in poetic register). خواندند is the past plural 'they uttered/they sang/they called out' and introduces the acclamation آفرین ('Bravo! / Well done!') which is the direct speech/acclamation following the verb.
segments.s736-g128: بران is an adverbial/allocutive 'upon him / to that one' linking the acclamation to its recipient. پر هنر is a predicate-adjective construction meaning 'full of skill' (پر = 'full (of)', هنر = 'skill/virtue'), and شهریار زمین is an appositive 'the king / sovereign of the earth' that identifies the person receiving the praise.
segments.s736-g81: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing source/origin. The phrase 'کین نو آیین و کین کهن' strings two genitive noun phrases together: 'کین نو آیین' = 'hatred of the new/custom' (i.e. newly instituted hatred) and 'کین کهن' = 'ancient hatred.' Word order is noun + modifier (کین + نو آیین / کهن). The conjunction و simply links the two kinds of 'کین.'
segments.s736-g82: مگر here introduces the main clause with a rhetorical/optative flavor (classical use akin to 'perhaps' or 'would that'), followed by locative در 'in' + جهان 'world.' تازه is an adjective modifying سخن (placed before the verb), and گردد is the classical third-person present subjunctive/passive 'become' (گردیدن/گشتن). The clause as a whole means 'may speech become new in the world.'
segments.s736-g83: سخنها is the plural 'words/tales' and functions as the direct topic. که here is the relative/connector 'that/which' introducing the subordinate clause 'I asked of your daughter.' پرسیدم is past first person 'I asked' and از introduces the source/agent 'from/of your daughter' (دخترت). The word order is topicalized object (سخنها) + relative clause.
segments.s736-g84: چنان دان is an imperative/directive: چنان 'thus' + دان (here imperative of 'to consider/know') = 'consider it so' or 'know it thus.' که introduces the content clause 'that ...' او is 'she,' تازه is an adverb/adjective 'newly' or 'just now,' کرد is the past verb 'made/did,' and افسرت (af-sarat) is your helmet/casque (here used like a crown). Together: 'Know it thus: she has newly made your casque.'
segments.s736-g85: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (ب + این), introducing the manner. مسیحا is vocative ('O Messiah') addressing someone, and بکوشد is the classical present/subjunctive third-person form 'strive/endeavour.' همی is an archaic particle attesting continuous/affirmative aspect ('still/indeed'). The clause reads as an exhortation addressed to the Messiah: 'By this, O Messiah, strive.'
segments.s736-g86: سخنهای ما is 'our words/tales' (possessive following the noun). کم here functions as 'little/less' (adverbial), and نیوشد is the classical form meaning 'is heard' (from نیوشیدن used for 'to hear/receive news'). The negative sense 'کم نیوشد' = 'is not little-heard' i.e. 'will not be heard less' or more idiomatically 'may our words not cease to be heard.' همی again adds an emphatic/continuous tone.
segments.s736-g87: به آرام is a prepositional phrase 'in/with calm' (or 'may (he/it) be calm'); شادست is a nominalized predicate meaning 'is joyful' (شاد + است fused in poetry), و links adjectives, and پیروزبخت is a compound adjective 'fortunate/prosperous.' These predicative adjectives describe the subject that follows.
segments.s736-g88: بدین here again is 'by/with this' (ب + این) or 'for this,' followed by خسروانی 'royal' qualifying the following nouns. نو آیین is 'new-rite / newly instituted custom' (نو modifies آیین), and درخت 'tree' is a metaphor for a dynasty/lineage. Together they form the subject: 'this royal tree of the new rite' — the thing wished to be calm, joyful and fortunate.
segments.s736-g89: همیشه (adverb) comes first and means 'always.' جهاندار is a title/noun 'sovereign, ruler' placed before یار تو ('your companion/friend'); یار تو is a possessive phrase with the pronoun تو after the noun (typical Persian order). باد here is not the noun 'wind' but the optative particle ‘may it be’ (classical/poetic usage) forming a blessing: 'May the sovereign always be your companion.'
segments.s736-g90: سر اختر is a genitive/epithet phrase where سر (here 'chief; leader') qualifies اختر ('star') — 'the chief/foremost star.' اندر کنار تو is a locative phrase 'at/beside you' with اندر ('among/at') + کنار ('beside') + the pronoun تو. باد again is the optative particle 'may it be,' giving the second blessing: 'May the chief star be at your side.'
segments.s736-g91: نهادند is a past tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they placed/put.' The preposition بر ('on/upon') introduces the complement; نامه‌بر is the recipient/vehicle 'envoy/letter‑bearer.' مهر شاه is a genitive phrase 'the king's seal' (مهر 'seal' governed by شاه 'king'). Word order here is verb + complement phrases, typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s736-g92: همی‌داشت is an imperfect/continuous past (همی- prefix marks ongoing/action-in-progress in classical Persian) meaning 'kept/was holding.' The following string خراد برزین نگاه should be read as a nominal phrase: خراد ('wise', here adjectival) + برزین (proper name) + نگاه ('gaze'), i.e. 'Barzin's wise gaze' or 'the wise gaze of Barzin.' The subject is implicit (he/Barzin), so the clause means 'Barzin kept a wise look.'
segments.s736-g93: گشادند is here past/plural and functions as 'they opened' (classical form of گشودن). زان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). در گنج باز places the action 'in/into the treasure‑vault' with در ('in') + گنج ('treasure, here vault') and باز as 'open/again' (here indicating the vault was opened), so the whole phrase means 'then they opened again the treasure‑vault.'
segments.s736-g94: کجا is the interrogative 'where.' گرد کرد is a light-verb construction: گرد (root meaning 'around/to go about') plus the light verb کرد ('did/made') giving 'went/turned about' or 'went off.' او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he.' به‌روز دراز is a temporal phrase 'on the long day' or 'that long day' (به‌روز = 'of the day/by day', دراز = 'long'), so the line asks 'Where did he go on that long day?'
segments.s736-g95: نخستین 'first' is an ordinal/adjective modifying the following numeral phrase. صد و شست literally 'one hundred and sixty' (classical numeric expression: صد 'hundred' + و 'and' + شست 'sixty'); note that the listed senses did not supply the numeric sense for شست, so that specific numeral sense is not among the provided options. بنداوسی is a proper name placed after the number, meaning 'the Bendausi' (a group or name).
segments.s736-g96: که introduces a relative clause 'whom/that.' پنداوسی is another form/variant of the proper name (here referenced in the relative clause). خواندش is the classical past transitive 'called him' — خواند + object clitic -ش (he called him). پارسی here denotes the Persians/the Persian people (ethnic adjective used substantively) and indicates the agent: 'whom the Persians called Pendausi.'
segments.s736-g33: 'دگر هرچ گفتی ز شیروی من' = a compact clause meaning 'whatever else was said about my lion-like one'. دگر هرچ (literally 'other/again whatever') is an emphatic way to introduce 'anything else'; گفتی here functions as a nominalized/abstract 'words/what was said' (rather than a present 2nd‑person verb), and ز is the poetic preposition 'of/about'. شیروی من puts the adjective/epithet شیروی ('lion-like, brave') before the possessive pronoun من (my) in a poetic order.
segments.s736-g34: 'ازان پاک تن پشت و نیروی من' = 'from that pure body (are) my support and my strength.' ازان ('from that') introduces a source; پاک تن is an adjective + noun ('pure body') in the usual modifier‑before‑noun order; پشت و نیروی من are two coordinated nouns followed by the independent possessive من — a poetic construction meaning 'my support and my strength' (the verb is omitted and supplied by context: 'come / are').
segments.s736-g35: 'بدانستم و آفرین خواندم' = 'I knew that and (I) cried "afarin".' بدانستم is past 1st‑person 'I knew' (the short prefix بـ + دانستم is typical in older/poetic Persian). آفرین خواندم uses خواندم (past 1st‑person 'I read/recited') with آفرین as the quoted exclamation; together the phrase means 'I exclaimed/praised (it)'.
segments.s736-g36: 'بران دین تو را پاک دین خواندم' = 'on account of that faith I called you pure in religion.' بران ('on/for that') gives the reason; دین following it names the thing ('that faith'). تو را with the object marker را marks 'you' as the direct object of خواندم; پاک دین is the predicate noun/epithet applied to 'you' and خواندم here is best read as 'I called/named' (to call someone something), i.e. sense 'to call'.
segments.s736-g37: 'دگر هرچ گفتی ز پاکیزه دین' = parallel to the earlier line: 'whatever else you said about the pure faith.' دگر هرچ introduces 'anything else/whatever else'; گفتی again functions as 'what was said/words' (abstract noun), and ز + پاکیزه دین links it to 'the pure religion/faith' (پاکیزه = pure/clean used adjectivally before دین).
segments.s736-g38: 'ز یکشنبدی روزه به آفرین' = 'with regard to/from the Sunday fast (I uttered) "afarin".' The initial ز is the causal/relational 'concerning' or 'because of'; یکشنبدی here marks 'Sunday' or 'Sunday‑associated' (an adjective/attributive), روزه is 'fast (the fast day)', and به آفرین ('to/with "afarin"') states the reaction (afarin = exclamation of praise). The clause is elliptical: the verb ('I said/cried') is supplied from the previous clause.
segments.s736-g39: 'همه خواند بر ما یکایک دبیر' = 'the scribe recited everything to us, one by one.' همه is 'all/everything' (the object); خواند is past 3rd‑person 'he/it read/recited'; بر ما = 'to us' (بر + ما); یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'; دبیر (postverbal here) is the subject 'the scribe/recorder' — in Persian poetry the subject often follows the verb.
segments.s736-g40: 'سخنهای بایسته و دلپذیر' = 'the necessary/proper and pleasant words.' سخنهای is the plural/construct 'words/sayings'; بایسته is an adjective meaning 'appropriate/proper/necessary' and دلپذیر 'pleasant'. Adjectives follow or precede nouns in poetry; here they qualify 'speakings' and complete the object of خواند.
segments.s736-g49: در اندیشه دل (literally “in the thought of the heart”) is a prepositional noun phrase; نگنجد is a present negative verb (ن + گنجَد) meaning “does not fit / is not contained.” In this poetic word order the predicate (نگنجد) comes before the grammatical subject خدای, so the clause reads “God does not fit into the heart’s thought.”
segments.s736-g50: به هستی = “to / in existence”; همو is an emphatic pronoun “he himself.” باشدت is an archaic/poetic form: باشد + enclitic ـت (to you) giving an optative/benefactive sense (“may he be to you” / “may he be your…”). رهنمای is the predicate noun “guide.” Together: “May he himself be your guide in existence.”
segments.s736-g51: دگر = “another.” کت here is a poetic/ambiguous noun (often ‘book’ or ‘coat’); in this context it functions as ‘tale/book.’ ز = poetic preposition “from”; دار = “house/abode”; مسیحا = “the Messiah.” سخن appended after this phrase functions as apposition: “a (piece of) speech/tale concerning the Messiah’s house.” The whole phrase is a nominal unit: “another tale from the house of the Messiah.”
segments.s736-g52: بیاد آمد is a fixed verbal phrase meaning “came to mind / was recalled” (بیاد = ‘into memory’ + آمد ‘came’). از روزگار کهن = “from/of ancient times”; this is an adverbial phrase specifying the origin/time of the recalled tale. آمد is simple past here, so: “it came to mind from ancient days.”
segments.s736-g53: مدان is an archaic/poetic term here used as a prohibitive/introductory exhortation meaning roughly “do not suppose / do not think.” دین is the topic “religion.” که باشد به خوبی بپای is a relative/subordinate clause: که = “that,” باشد = present/optative form “is/should be,” به خوبی = “by goodness,” and بپای (بـ + پای) is an older verb form meaning “to persist / to stand.” Altogether: “Do not suppose that the religion stands by goodness alone.”
segments.s736-g54: بدان here functions locatively as “in/with that” (classical contraction); دین = “religion.” نباشد is the negative present used as a general statement “is not / does not exist.” خرد رهنمای = “wisdom (is a) guide.” The clause means “In that religion wisdom is not the guide.” Note the negative predicate follows the subject, typical of classical poetic word order.
segments.s736-g55: کسی را که = “whomever” (کسی + object marker را + relative که). خوانی is 2nd-person present “you call / you name,” here used in the relative clause “whom you call …” همی is an archaic particle adding emphasis (≈ “indeed/just”), and سوگوار = “mourning / a mourner.” The phrase reads “whomsoever you indeed call a mourner.”
segments.s736-g56: که here introduces the causal/explanatory clause “for/because.” کردند is past plural “they did / they made / they caused.” پیغمبرش را = “his prophet” (object, marked by را). بدار in this context means “to the gallows / hanged”; کرد(ند) + بدار is a classical causative/perfective construction meaning “they hanged (him).” So the clause gives the reason: “because they hanged his prophet.”
segments.s736-g65: از ایران is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb to mark origin ('from Iran'). چو here functions as a temporal subordinating conjunction 'when/if' introducing the clause. فرستم and بروم are first-person classical Persian verb forms (subjunctive/optative/present) stacked together to express a hypothetical or future action: 'I send ... and I go.' چوبی is an adjective (literally 'wooden') describing the thing sent; word order places the origin phrase, the subordinate marker, the adjective/object, then the verbs.
segments.s736-g66: بخندد is a third-person singular present/subjunctive form here meaning 'will laugh' or 'laugh at' (an agentive or impersonal 'it/they will deride'). بما is the oblique form 'at/to us'. بر introduces the locative/covering phrase بر همه مرز و بوم — 'over/throughout all borders and homeland'; همه modifies مرز و بوم as 'all/every'.
segments.s736-g67: به موبد نباید is an impersonal construction: به + role (موبد) + نباید ('it is not proper/should not be') — literally 'for a mobed it is not fitting.' که introduces the subordinate clause explaining what is not fitting. ترسا شدم is the predicate of that clause: ترسا (a pious/Christian person) + شدم (past 'I became').
segments.s736-g68: گر is the conditional 'if.' از بهر (a fixed idiom) means 'for the sake of / because of,' so گر از بهر مریم ... شدم = 'if I became ... for Mary.' سکوبا is an obscure/rare lexical item here (left untranslated in the line); شدم is the past-tense verb 'I became.' The whole segment gives a counterfactual concessive condition.
segments.s736-g69: دگر here means 'other/again' or 'further' and precedes the noun آرزو ('desire'). هرچ ('whatever') qualifies آرزو; the modal باید ('must/ought') governs the verb بخواه. بخواه is the present/jussive form 'seek/ask (for)' — in context it's an instruction or allowance: 'ask for whatever else you must.' Word order is somewhat inverted (adverbial/qualifier before verb) as typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s736-g70: شمار here is a noun 'number' used loosely to mean 'many' or 'as to numbers'; سوی ما is the prepositional phrase 'toward us/at our side.' گشادست is the literary contracted form of 'گشاده‌است' ('is open/wide') and serves as the predicate modifying راه ('road/way'), which comes at the end: 'the way to us is open.' This is a declarative reassurance following the injunction.
segments.s736-g71: پسندیدم is the simple past first-person 'I liked/approved.' آن هدیه‌های تو is the direct object: آن ('those/that') + هدیه‌های (plural 'gifts') + تو ('your'). نیز 'also' follows the object for emphasis: 'I liked those gifts of yours too.'
segments.s736-g72: کجا is an interrogative 'where' used rhetorically (here: 'how/where did you...'). رنج بردی is a light-verb construction meaning 'you bore/suffered hardship' (رنج + بردی from بردن). ز is the poetic variant of از ('from'), and هر گونه چیز ('every kind of thing') is the object of that suffering: 'for/from every sort of thing.' The sequence is a rhetorical question about what hardships were endured.
